title: Ārya-Mañjuśrī-Nāma-Saṅgīti — Chanting the Names of Noble Mañjuśrī
subtitle: A Buddhist tantric liturgical text from the words of the Buddha, chanted at Lhungtok Choekhorling Monastery. Tibetan text with English translation.

summary.one_line: The Ārya-Mañjuśrī-Nāma-Saṅgīti ('Chanting the Names of Noble Mañjuśrī') is a Buddhist tantric text in which Mañjuśrī is invoked through a series of epithets and mantras, revealing the nature of enl...
summary.scope: Covers the full liturgical text of the Mañjuśrī-nāma-saṅgīti in Tibetan (Wylie transliteration) and English translation. Mañjuśrī as the bodhisattva of wisdom, Vajradhara as the source, the homages to the five Buddha families, and the mantra recitation. The text is chanted as a daily practice in Tibetan Buddhist monasteries.

sources.label: Ārya-Mañjuśrī-Nāma-Saṅgīti — Chanting the Names of Noble Mañjuśrī (Lhungtok Choekhorling Monastery edition). PDF with Tibetan and English.

body.text: The text begins with homage to ever-youthful Mañjuśrī (mañjuśrī kumārabhūta), setting the tone of devotional veneration. Vajradhara is invoked as the source — 'Vajradhara, ever glorious, supreme subduer of the hard to tame, the hero conquering the triple world.' Mañjuśrī embodies the wisdom of all Buddhas, and chanting his names is both a practice of devotion and a meditation on the nature of enlightened cognition. The text is traditionally chanted as a daily sadhana in Tibetan Buddhist monasteries, particularly in the Gelug and Kagyü traditions.

Mañjuśrī is the bodhisattva of wisdom (prajñā), depicted with a sword cutting through ignorance and a text (the Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra) on a lotus at his heart. His name means 'Gentle Glory' — the union of gentleness (the compassion of all Buddhas) and glory (the wisdom of all Buddhas). In the tantric context, Mañjuśrī is not merely a deity but the personification of enlightened intelligence itself. Chanting his names is a method of realizing that one's own mind, at its deepest level, IS Mañjuśrī.

The text connects Mañjuśrī to the five Buddha families (pañcatathāgata): Vairocana (center, dharma-dhātu wisdom), Akṣobhya (east, mirror-like wisdom), Ratnasambhava (south, equalizing wisdom), Amitābha (west, discriminating wisdom), Amoghasiddhi (north, all-accomplishing wisdom). Each of Mañjuśrī's aspects corresponds to one of these wisdoms, and chanting his names activates the corresponding wisdom in the practitioner. This maps to the five wisdoms of the Chakrasambhara Tantra (Avalon ed.) and the transformation of the five skandhas into the five Buddhas.

gaps: Full Tibetan text extraction; complete English translation; ritual instructions for chanting

issues.title: Full Tibetan text extraction needed
issues.description: The PDF contains Tibetan text that could not be fully extracted. The RO currently covers the English portions only.

versions.summary: Initial RO created for the Mañjuśrī-nāma-saṅgīti chanting text. 3 passages covering the invocation, Mañjuśrī as wisdom, and the five Buddha families.

timeline.event: RO created from liturgical PDF (540K, 78 pages). Tibetan/English chanting text.
